title: Painting skirting boards and architrave
subtitle: Sand, prime bare wood, cut in clean
excerpt: The sequence for painting skirting boards and door architrave in gloss or satin finish: sanding back the existing surface, priming any bare areas, cutting in a clean edge against the wall, applying topcoat, and removing tape before the paint skins over.
sourceNotes: Standard UK decorating practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual and the Dulux Technical Data Sheets for oil-based and water-based gloss and satin finishes.

recipeTools.notes: For tight corners and moulding detail.
For removing loose or flaking paint before sanding.
For small dents or nail holes in the wood.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of painting a clean edge along skirting, architrave, ceiling, or window-bar with a brush, before rolling the bulk of the wall. The skill that separates a good paint job from a rolled one.

content.text: Skirting boards and architrave show every brush mark and drip because the eye is drawn to the edge between the white woodwork and the wall colour. Good preparation and slow, deliberate
cutting in
account for most of the result. The paint type matters less than these two steps.
Paint types

Water-based gloss and satin (such as Dulux Quick Dry) dry in 2 to 4 hours, are low-odour, and clean up with water. Oil-based gloss takes 12 to 16 hours to dry but gives a harder finish. For most domestic work the water-based product is the better choice; the finish is comparable and the faster re-coat time matters in a room being used. Either can go over the other provided the existing coat is sound and abraded.
Preparation
Step 1, clean the woodwork.
Wipe skirting and architrave with a damp cloth to remove dust and grease. Grease under gloss shows as a fisheye or causes the new paint to peel.
Step 2, scrape back any loose paint.

Use the scraper to remove any paint that is already lifting or flaking. Feather the edges of any stripped areas with 80-grit sandpaper on the sanding block. Fill any dents or nail pops with filler, allow to cure, and sand flush.
Step 3, sand the whole surface.

Sand the existing paintwork lightly with 120-grit to give the topcoat a key. This is called deglossing: the topcoat will not bond reliably to a glossy surface without it. Sand along the grain of the wood. Wipe off the dust.
Step 4, prime any bare wood.

Any areas where the old paint was scraped back to bare wood must be primed before the topcoat. Use the matching primer for the topcoat type. Allow the primer to dry fully. Sand lightly with 180-grit and wipe clean.
Step 5, apply masking tape to the wall.

Run decorator's tape along the wall just above the skirting and alongside the architrave where it meets the wall. Press the edge firmly with a fingernail. Masking tape protects the wall and allows a slightly less careful brush stroke; even with tape, cutting in is still the key to a sharp line.
Painting
Step 6, apply the first topcoat.

Load the 50 mm brush to about half the bristle length. Start each stroke from the unpainted area, drawing into the wet paint rather than from the wet paint outward. Paint the full length of a skirting run or an architrave leg in one continuous session without stopping; a join in a gloss or satin finish is visible even under the second coat.
Step 7, remove the masking tape.

Remove the tape while the paint is still slightly wet, pulling it at 45 degrees away from the painted surface. If the paint has dried, score along the tape edge with a knife before pulling to avoid lifting the dried film.
Step 8, apply the second coat.

Allow the first coat to dry completely, then sand lightly with 180-grit and wipe clean. Re-tape if needed. Apply the second coat in the same direction as the first. Two coats of water-based product and three coats of oil-based product is the standard for a durable finish.
Common mistakes
Brush marks in the dried paint.

Oil-based gloss in particular shows every stroke if the paint is worked too hard after it begins to set. Load the brush, lay the paint on with minimal strokes, and do not go back once the paint begins to set.
Runs and drips.
Too much paint on the brush, or a brush loaded on the ferrule rather than the tip. Scrape back any runs once dry with 120-grit, re-prime if the scrape goes to bare wood, and re-coat.
Ragged line at the wall edge.

The tape was not pressed firmly at the edge, or it was removed after the paint had cured. For a re-do, let it dry fully, scrape back the ragged line, re-prime bare plaster, and re-paint with the tape well pressed.
